FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), N=8753 10-14 year old early adolescents

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone variables: time on phone (MPIQ); qualifying social media variables: time on social media (SMAQ). Also includes video game use (VGAQ). Applies B1: separate β reported for each modality (video games, social media, mobile phone) with own coefficients.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses estimating associations between sociodemographic factors and problematic screen use measures (β coefficients with 95% CI reported in Table 3)

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports B coefficients with 95% CI and p-values for associations between sociodemographic predictors and all three problematic screen use outcomes

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD data examining sociodemographic associations with smartphone/social media use outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplicate identified; published primary analysis in Pediatric Research 2022

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails condition (a): SM and smartphone/mobile phone are dependent variables in analyses, BUT paper also includes analyses where screen use (SM/phone/games) are outcomes being predicted by sociodemographic factors, NOT as IVs predicting outcomes. However, FT-EC7 requires SM/smartphone as ONLY DV with NO SM→outcome analyses. This paper examines sociodemographic→SM/phone relationships but not SM→outcomes. Does not meet FT-EC7 because: condition (a) requires that SM is DV in EVERY analysis AND paper has NO SM→non-SM outcome analyses. Here SM is DV only; no SM→outcome analyses present. Condition (b) met: all predictors are demographic (age, sex, race, income, education, marital status). Condition (c) met: cross-sectional design. FT-EC7 DOES NOT APPLY because this is sociodemographic→SM relationship (condition a satisfied but b/c are met but overall logic: SM as sole DV, no SM as IV to outcomes, demographics only as predictors = ecological not causal). Applies B15: cross-sectional sociodemographic→SM analysis, INCLUDE but NOT EC7.
notes: Multimodal analysis of video games, social media, and mobile phone problematic use with robust sociodemographic correlates. Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. FT-EC7 structure met but does not qualify as exclusion criterion per B15 carve-out.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, baseline data (N=4,042), 9-10 year old children

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity included as predictor variable in machine learning models. Abstract lists 'screen media activity' as key predictive variable. Qualifies as SM/smartphone exposure variable per B1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning predictive modeling with elastic net, random forest, light gradient boosting, and support vector regression. Cross-validation approach provides model performance metrics and coefficient-based feature importance.

FT_IC4.evidence: Machine learning models report feature importance; applies B8 rule: 'Penalized regression β (Lasso/Ridge/elastic-net) counts.' Elastic net regression explicitly used, permitting extraction of regularized coefficients from main models.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical machine learning analysis of ABCD baseline data predicting DRD at follow-up from baseline variables including screen media activity

FT_IC6.evidence: Published primary analysis in Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology 2022, no duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails condition (a) of FT-EC7: Screen media activity is included as a PREDICTOR variable, not as the dependent variable. The dependent variable is delayed reward discounting (DRD), a neuropsychological outcome. FT-EC7 requires smartphone/SM to be the DEPENDENT variable in every analysis with NO SM→outcome analyses. Here SM is an IV predicting DRD (non-SM outcome), violating condition (a).
notes: Prediction study using ABCD baseline to forecast DRD at 1-year follow-up. Screen media activity is one of many predictors alongside neuropsych, psychopathology, parenting, personality. ML models with elastic net and cross-validation meet IC3/IC4. Does not qualify as FT-EC7 exclusion.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study baseline (2016-2018), N=10,650 children ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone/social media variables: children's reported use of TV, videos, video games, and social media; mobile phone involvement measured. Includes mature-rated media use (mature-rated movies, mature-rated games). Applies B1: separate associations reported for TV, videos, gaming, social media.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel general linear models and multilevel logistic regression analyses examining associations between parental psychological problems and children's screen time outcomes. Models include β coefficients and odds ratios with 95% CI.

FT_IC4.evidence: Tables 2, 3, 4 report regression coefficients with 95% CI, odds ratios with 95% CI, and p-values for associations between parental variables and screen time/mature media outcomes

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD baseline data examining associations between parental psychological problems (ASR) and children's screen use behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published primary analysis in Acta Paediatrica 2022, no duplicate identified

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails condition (a): Parental psychological problems (NOT smartphone/SM demographics) are the independent variables. Children's screen time and mature media use are dependent variables. The paper examines parental mental health→child SM/screen outcomes, not demographics→SM. Additionally, condition (b) fails: key predictor is parental psychological problems (depression, anxiety, internalizing/externalizing), which is explicitly listed in NOT EC7 rules (B17: 'ACEs, parenting, parental psychopathology, family conflict, sleep, depression, anxiety, ADHD...' disqualify). Parental psychopathology is a direct predictor, violating B17.
notes: Examines parental mental health as predictor of children's screen behaviors. Parental psychological problems (depression, anxiety) as predictors disqualify from FT-EC7 per B17. Original analysis with valid regression models reporting separate SM/phone/gaming associations.
